steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: Left side: "the average summer temperature" — a noun phrase serving as the subject. Right side: "just 66.7 degrees Fahrenheit" — a noun phrase serving as the complement, not an independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
Structure: "the average summer temperature [blank] just 66.7 degrees Fahrenheit." Subject = "the average summer temperature"; complement = "just 66.7 degrees Fahrenheit." These need only a verb to connect them.

Rule: a linking verb connects directly to its subject complement—no punctuation between them.

A. is — the bare verb "is" links subject to complement with no interruption. "The average summer temperature is just 66.7 degrees Fahrenheit" is a complete, correct sentence. Correct.

B. is; — a semicolon must be followed by an independent clause. "Just 66.7 degrees Fahrenheit" has no verb, so it cannot stand alone. Eliminated.

C. is: — a colon after "is" requires a complete independent clause before it ("the average summer temperature is" is not complete). Eliminated.

D. is, — inserting a comma between a linking verb and its complement incorrectly interrupts the verb-complement relationship; commas do not belong between a linking verb and its complement. Eliminated.

A is correct.
